title: NIS2 Art.12/21 secure development and vulnerability disclosure evidence

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.12 receives partial assurance because UC evidence proves the monitorable workflow or telemetry portion while legal approval and filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.12 using NIS2 Art.12/21 secure development and vulnerability disclosure evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search for NIS2 Art.12/21 secure development and vulnerability disclosure evidence, dashboard panel, and archived result export in index=audit_evidence for Art.12.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(e) receives partial assurance because UC evidence proves the monitorable workflow or telemetry portion while legal approval and filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.21(2)(e) using NIS2 Art.12/21 secure development and vulnerability disclosure evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search for NIS2 Art.12/21 secure development and vulnerability disclosure evidence, dashboard panel, and archived result export in index=audit_evidence for Art.21(2)(e).

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(f) receives partial assurance because UC evidence proves the monitorable workflow or telemetry portion while legal approval and filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.21(2)(f) using NIS2 Art.12/21 secure development and vulnerability disclosure evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search for NIS2 Art.12/21 secure development and vulnerability disclosure evidence, dashboard panel, and archived result export in index=audit_evidence for Art.21(2)(f).

dataSources: `index=devops` `sourcetype=github:audit` with `action`, `repo`, `actor`, `created_at` from Splunk Add-on for GitHub ([Splunkbase 5598](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5598)); `index=sast` `sourcetype=sonarqube:issues` with `severity`, `status`, `project_key`; `index=vuln` for scanner SLA context; `lookup=vulnerability_disclosure_intake.csv` tracking `reporter`, `disclosure_status`, `due_date`.
app: Splunk Add-on for GitHub ([Splunkbase 5598](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5598)); Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud for HEC ingestion of SonarQube webhooks.
spl:
| inputlookup vulnerability_disclosure_intake.csv
| eval due_epoch=strptime(due_date,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval intake_overdue=if(disclosure_status!="closed" AND now()>due_epoch,1,0)
| where intake_overdue=1
| eval finding_type="disclosure_overdue", asset=coalesce(disclosure_id,reporter_email)
| fields finding_type, asset, disclosure_status, due_date
| append [
    search index=sast sourcetype=sonarqube:issues severity IN ("BLOCKER","CRITICAL") status=OPEN earliest=-7d
    | stats dc(key) as open_findings by project_key
    | eval finding_type="sast_open_critical", asset=project_key
]
| append [
    search index=devops sourcetype=github:audit earliest=-7d action="repo.access" OR action="protected_branch.*"
    | search NOT match(actor,"^(svc-|github-app)")
    | stats dc(repo) as repos_touched by actor
    | where repos_touched>15
    | eval finding_type="audit_volume_spike", asset=actor
]
| lookup nis2_secdv_kfp_exceptions asset OUTPUT exception_until
| where isnull(exception_until) OR now()>strptime(exception_until,"%Y-%m-%d")
| table finding_type, asset, disclosure_status, due_date, open_findings, repos_touched
description: Combines GitHub organisation audit events, open SonarQube blocker and critical findings, and CSV-tracked vulnerability-disclosure tickets so engineering gates and external reporter SLAs stay visible to compliance.
value: Product and legal leadership receive a single operational rhythm for secure build controls and coordinated disclosure rather than waiting for annual questionnaires to uncover stale scanner noise or unanswered researcher emails.
implementation: (1) Install Splunkbase 5598 and connect the GitHub App with least privilege. (2) Send SonarQube issues via HEC. (3) Maintain `vulnerability_disclosure_intake.csv` with legal. (4) Alert weekly; attach owners per `project_key`. (5) Archive to `index=audit_evidence`.
visualization: Stacked bar of disclosure states, table of open critical SAST issues, timeline of `github:audit` branch events.

references.title: Splunk Docs — Configure Splunk Connect for GitHub (HEC pattern)

knownFalsePositives:
• **Canary repos excluded from branch policies** — Distinguish: repository name matches `^lab-`. Suppress: `lookup nis2_secdv_kfp_exceptions` listing allowed repos with `exception_until`.
• **SonarQube noise in generated code** — Distinguish: `component` path includes `/generated/`. Suppress: filter `where NOT match(component,"/generated/")` after validating with appsec.
• **Duplicate HEC events** — Distinguish: `_raw` hash repeats within 60 seconds. Suppress: `dedup 60 keeplog=false` in subsearch.
• **Emergency break-glass merges during incident** — Distinguish: `incident_id` tagged in commit message. Suppress: join `break_glass_register` CSV with 24-hour auto expiry.
exclusions: Does not perform legal triage of vulnerability reports, sign safe harbour statements, or certify pen-test scopes; does not replace secure SDLC policy.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.51` output, correlated `github:audit` and `sonarqube:issues` events, disclosure CSV snapshots, and sealed export in `index=audit_evidence`.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Seed `vulnerability_disclosure_intake.csv` with `disclosure_status=open` and a `due_date` in the past, then run the search to confirm the intake branch surfaces the overdue `disclosure_id` while a parallel SonarQube fixture populates `count>0` for `project_key=demo_service`.
controlTest.negativeScenario: When every disclosure row is `closed` ahead of schedule and no open blocker SonarQube issues exist for monitored projects, the combined result set is empty except for deliberately injected test traffic that you filter out in the lab.

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• This UC is a composite evidence search that combines three distinct detection signals into a single NIS2 Art.12 + Art.21(2)(e) + Art.21(2)(f) evidence package: (1) overdue vulnerability disclosure intake tickets (Art.12 — coordinated vulnerability disclosure), (2) open blocker/critical SonarQube findings (Art.21(2)(e) — security in development and maintenance), and (3) GitHub organisation audit events indicating unusual repository access or branch protection changes (Art.21(2)(f) — policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk-management measures). It is designed for organisations that want a single weekly search covering the full secure development and vulnerability disclosure spectrum.
• Delineation from UC-22.2.15 and UC-22.2.27: UC-22.2.15 measures pipeline scan coverage (quantitative — what percentage of pipelines include scanning). UC-22.2.27 measures vulnerability disclosure triage SLA (time-based — how fast disclosures are triaged). This UC (22.2.51) is qualitative and multi-dimensional: it surfaces specific findings that need attention — individual overdue disclosures, individual open critical SAST issues, and individual suspicious audit events. Together, the three UCs provide comprehensive Art.21(2)(e) evidence at different levels of abstraction.
• **Vulnerability disclosure intake CSV**: `vulnerability_disclosure_intake.csv` with columns: `disclosure_id` (unique identifier, e.g., VD-2026-001), `reporter_email` (the external researcher's email — handle as PII under GDPR), `disclosure_status` (open, in_triage, in_remediation, closed, not_applicable), `due_date` (ISO 8601 date — the deadline for closing or formally responding to the disclosure), `affected_product` (the product or service affected), `severity_estimate` (critical/high/medium/low — initial severity estimate), `assigned_analyst` (the internal analyst responsible). This CSV is maintained by the VD coordinator (typically the Head of IR or a designated security analyst). It is distinct from ServiceNow incidents — some organisations track disclosures in a dedicated CSV because ServiceNow may not support the full CVD workflow (researcher communication, coordinated disclosure timing, embargo management).
• Splunk Add-on for GitHub (Splunkbase 5598) configured to ingest organisation audit log events to `index=devops sourcetype=github:audit`. GitHub Enterprise and GitHub Enterprise Cloud provide audit log streaming. Key event types:
  - `repo.access` — a user accessed a repository (indicates repository browsing or cloning)
  - `protected_branch.create` / `protected_branch.destroy` / `protected_branch.update_*` — changes to branch protection rules (adding or removing required reviews, status checks, etc.)
  - `repo.config.disable_fork` / `repo.config.enable_fork` — fork policy changes
  - `org.add_member` / `org.remove_member` — organisation membership changes
  The SPL filters to `action="repo.access" OR action="protected_branch.*"` to focus on access patterns and security configuration changes.
• SonarQube integration: SonarQube Server webhooks configured to POST analysis results to a Splunk HEC endpoint. In SonarQube: Administration > Configuration > Webhooks > Create. URL: `https://hec.splunk.example.com:8088/services/collector`. Secret: a shared secret for HMAC validation. Alternatively, use a scripted input to poll SonarQube's Issues API: `GET /api/issues/search?statuses=OPEN&severities=BLOCKER,CRITICAL&ps=500`. Events land in `index=sast sourcetype=sonarqube:issues`. Key fields: `key` (unique issue identifier), `severity` (BLOCKER, CRITICAL, MAJOR, MINOR, INFO), `status` (OPEN, CONFIRMED, REOPENED, RESOLVED, CLOSED), `project_key` (the SonarQube project — typically maps to a repository name), `component` (the file path within the project), `message` (description of the finding), `creationDate` (when the issue was first detected).
• Exception handling: `nis2_secdv_kfp_exceptions.csv` with columns `asset` (the disclosure_id, project_key, or actor being excepted), `exception_until` (ISO 8601 date), `reason`. This handles: (a) disclosures awaiting coordinated publication dates (embargo), (b) SonarQube findings in generated code that cannot be fixed, (c) bot accounts that legitimately access many repositories (e.g., `dependabot[bot]`, `renovate[bot]`).
• Index requirements: `index=devops` ≥ 90 days, `index=sast` ≥ 90 days, `index=audit_evidence` ≥ 7 years.
• RBAC: role `nis2_secdv_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = devops, sast, audit_evidence` and write access to the `vulnerability_disclosure_intake.csv` lookup. This role must be restricted — the VD CSV contains researcher contact information (PII) and unpatched vulnerability details.
• GitHub Enterprise licensing: the `github:audit` sourcetype requires GitHub Enterprise Cloud (with audit log streaming) or GitHub Enterprise Server (with audit log API). GitHub Free and GitHub Team plans do not provide organisation-level audit logs. If your organisation uses GitHub Free, remove the third `append` block from the SPL.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**Vulnerability disclosure intake CSV:**
Place `vulnerability_disclosure_intake.csv` in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-secdv/lookups/`. Define in `transforms.conf`:
```ini
[vulnerability_disclosure_intake]
filename = vulnerability_disclosure_intake.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
```
The CSV is maintained manually by the VD coordinator. Populate with test data to validate the search before going live. Typical lifecycle: a disclosure is received via email or bug bounty platform → added to the CSV with `disclosure_status=open` and `due_date` set to 90 days from receipt (standard CVD timeline) → analyst triages and changes status to `in_triage` → remediation begins (`in_remediation`) → fix deployed, disclosure closed (`closed`).

Validate: `| inputlookup vulnerability_disclosure_intake.csv | stats count dc(disclosure_id) as disclosures dc(disclosure_status) as statuses values(disclosure_status) as status_list`. Verify `disclosures > 0` and `status_list` contains expected values.

**SonarQube issues feed:**
In SonarQube: Administration > Configuration > Webhooks > Create:
- Name: `Splunk HEC`
- URL: `https://hec.splunk.example.com:8088/services/collector`
- Secret: `<shared-secret>`

SonarQube sends a webhook on every project analysis completion. The payload contains the project key, quality gate status, and new issues found. For historical issues (already existing before webhook setup), run a one-time backfill: `GET /api/issues/search?statuses=OPEN&severities=BLOCKER,CRITICAL&ps=500` and POST the results to HEC.

Configure props.conf for field extraction:
```ini
[sonarqube:issues]
KV_MODE = json
TIME_FORMAT = %Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z
TIME_PREFIX = "creationDate":"
FIELDALIAS-project = project AS project_key
FIELDALIAS-issue_key = key AS key
```

Verify: `index=sast sourcetype=sonarqube:issues severity IN ("BLOCKER","CRITICAL") status=OPEN earliest=-7d | stats count dc(key) as issues dc(project_key) as projects`. Expected: `issues` matches the count in SonarQube's Issues tab filtered to Blocker+Critical, Status=Open.

**GitHub audit log:**
Install the Splunk Add-on for GitHub (Splunkbase 5598). Configure the audit log input:
- API endpoint: `api.github.com/orgs/{org}/audit-log` (or GHES URL)
- Authentication: PAT with `admin:org` and `audit_log` scopes
- Interval: 300 seconds
- Index: `devops`
- Sourcetype: `github:audit`

Verify: `index=devops sourcetype=github:audit earliest=-7d | stats count dc(action) as actions dc(actor) as actors`. Expected: `actions` shows various audit event types (10+ distinct actions), `actors` shows active users.

**Service account exclusion filter test:**
The SPL filters `NOT match(actor,"^(svc-|github-app)")` to exclude service accounts and GitHub Apps. Verify your service account naming convention: `index=devops sourcetype=github:audit | stats count by actor | sort - count | head 20`. If service accounts use a different naming pattern (e.g., `bot-`, `automation-`), update the regex.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.51 NIS2 Secure Development and Vulnerability Disclosure Evidence`.

Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

• **Three-branch architecture with `append`**: similar to UC-22.2.52, this search produces three independent result sets, each detecting a different Art.12/Art.21(2)(e)/Art.21(2)(f) finding:
  - **Branch 1 (inputlookup)**: overdue vulnerability disclosures from the CSV. `disclosure_status != "closed"` AND `now() > due_epoch` identifies disclosures that should have been resolved by their due date but remain open. These are Art.12 findings — the organisation has received a vulnerability report and has not responded within the committed timeline.
  - **Branch 2 (first append)**: open BLOCKER and CRITICAL SonarQube findings from the last 7 days. `dc(key)` counts unique issues per project. These are Art.21(2)(e) findings — known severe code quality or security issues that have not been remediated.
  - **Branch 3 (second append)**: GitHub audit events where a human user (not service account) accessed or modified branch protections across more than 15 repositories in 7 days. This is an Art.21(2)(f) anomaly — a single user touching many repositories' security configurations may indicate: (a) legitimate infrastructure work (DevOps engineer updating branch policies across all repos), (b) a compromised account performing reconnaissance, or (c) an insider disabling security controls before exfiltration.

• **`finding_type` and `asset` as uniform output fields**: each branch produces a `finding_type` label and an `asset` identifier, creating a uniform table schema across the three detection signals. This allows a single dashboard panel to display all findings regardless of source.

• **`repos_touched > 15` threshold (Branch 3)**: 15 repositories in 7 days is the anomaly threshold. A typical developer accesses 3-5 repositories per week. An administrator performing a policy update across all repos will legitimately exceed this — add them to the exception register during the change window. Tune based on your organisation's size: smaller orgs may use 10, larger orgs may use 25.

• **`NOT match(actor,"^(svc-|github-app)")` service account filter**: GitHub Apps (e.g., `dependabot[bot]`, `github-actions[bot]`) and service accounts (e.g., `svc-cicd`, `svc-terraform`) access many repositories as part of automation. Without this filter, they would always trigger the anomaly threshold. The regex uses `^(svc-|github-app)` — adjust to match your naming convention.

• **Exception register lookup**: applied at the end to filter known false positives. The `asset` field from all three branches is looked up against `nis2_secdv_kfp_exceptions.csv`. Expired exceptions (past `exception_until` date) are automatically re-included.

Schedule: cron `0 7 * * 1,4` (Monday and Thursday at 07:00 — twice-weekly cadence for engineering teams). Time range: use lookback windows embedded in the SPL (90d for VD intake via CSV, 7d for SonarQube, 7d for GitHub audit). Trigger: `Number of results > 0`.

Alert actions: (1) For `finding_type=disclosure_overdue`: PagerDuty page to the VD coordinator with `urgency=high`. Overdue disclosures are a direct NIS2 Art.12 compliance risk — researchers may publicly disclose the vulnerability if the organisation is unresponsive. (2) For `finding_type=sast_open_critical`: email to the engineering security lead with project name and issue count. (3) For `finding_type=audit_volume_spike`: email to the security operations team for investigation. (4) Write all findings to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:secdv:evidence`.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Disclosure overdue detection**: add a test row to `vulnerability_disclosure_intake.csv` with `disclosure_status=open`, `due_date` set to 7 days ago. Run the search. Confirm the row appears with `finding_type=disclosure_overdue`. Change `disclosure_status=closed`. Run again. Confirm the row no longer appears. Change `due_date` to 7 days in the future (open but not overdue). Run again. Confirm no appearance.

(b) **SonarQube finding detection**: in SonarQube, create a test project with a deliberate BLOCKER issue (e.g., a SQL injection finding in a test file). Trigger analysis. Wait for the webhook to deliver to HEC (typically 30-60 seconds). Run the search. Confirm the project appears with `finding_type=sast_open_critical` and `open_findings=1`.

(c) **GitHub audit anomaly detection**: this is harder to test in production. In a lab GitHub Enterprise instance, have a test user access 16 different repositories within a 7-day window. Run the search. Confirm the user appears with `finding_type=audit_volume_spike` and `repos_touched=16`. Verify that a GitHub App (e.g., `dependabot[bot]`) does NOT appear (filtered by the regex).

(d) **Exception register test**: add the test disclosure's `disclosure_id` to `nis2_secdv_kfp_exceptions.csv` with `exception_until=<tomorrow>`. Run the search. Confirm the disclosure no longer appears. Set `exception_until=<yesterday>`. Run again. Confirm it reappears (exception expired).

(e) **Negative test**: close all test disclosures, fix the SonarQube issue, and ensure no user exceeds the repo threshold. Run the search. Confirm zero results.

(f) **SonarQube project key alignment**: verify `project_key` in SonarQube matches the `repository` name used in UC-22.2.15. Run: `index=sast sourcetype=sonarqube:issues | stats count by project_key | head 20`. Compare with `index=devops | stats count by repository | head 20`. If naming conventions differ (e.g., SonarQube uses `com.example:service-a` while GitHub uses `org/service-a`), create a mapping lookup.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Secure Development and Disclosure Evidence`):
• **Row 1 — Headline tiles**: Single-value `Overdue disclosures` (red ≥ 1 — Art.12 compliance risk). Single-value `Open critical SAST findings` (amber ≥ 1). Single-value `Audit volume anomalies` (amber ≥ 1). Single-value `Total findings` (count from all three branches).
• **Row 2 — Finding type breakdown**: pie chart or stacked bar of `finding_type` showing the distribution across the three branches. If one branch dominates, focus remediation effort there.
• **Row 3 — Disclosure tracker**: table of all disclosure rows from the CSV with `disclosure_id`, `reporter_email` (masked for non-VD-coordinator roles), `disclosure_status`, `due_date`, `days_overdue` (calculated). Colour-code: green (within timeline), amber (due within 7 days), red (overdue).
• **Row 4 — SAST findings by project**: bar chart of `open_findings` by `project_key`, sorted descending. Shows which codebases have the worst SAST backlog.
• **Row 5 — GitHub audit timeline**: `index=devops sourcetype=github:audit action IN ("repo.access","protected_branch.*") earliest=-7d | timechart span=1h count by actor limit=10`. Shows audit event volume per user over the last week — spikes indicate concentrated activity.

Runbook (owner: CISO / Engineering Security):
1. Twice-weekly: review alert. For each finding:
   - `disclosure_overdue`: contact the VD coordinator immediately. Determine the disclosure status: is remediation in progress? Is the delay due to vendor dependency (waiting for upstream patch)? If the researcher has not received any communication, send an acknowledgement immediately and update the CSV. NIS2 Art.12 and the organisation's VDP commit to timely responses — overdue disclosures are reputational and compliance risks.
   - `sast_open_critical`: contact the engineering lead for the affected project. Determine if the finding is a true positive or a SonarQube false positive. If true positive: create a Jira ticket and prioritise remediation. If false positive: mark as "Won't Fix" in SonarQube with justification, and add to the exception register.
   - `audit_volume_spike`: investigate the user's activity. Review: `index=devops sourcetype=github:audit actor="<user>" earliest=-7d | table _time action repo`. Determine if the activity is legitimate (infrastructure change, repo migration) or suspicious (reconnaissance, control disabling). If legitimate: add a time-bound exception. If suspicious: escalate to the SOC.
2. Monthly: review the overall finding trend. Present to the CISO with: disclosure SLA compliance rate (from UC-22.2.27), pipeline scan coverage (from UC-22.2.15), and this UC's findings count. Together, these three UCs provide the complete Art.21(2)(e) evidence package.
3. Quarterly: archive dashboard PDF and CSV with RFC3161 signing.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **403 from GitHub audit log API.** The PAT lacks the `audit_log` scope (only available in GitHub Enterprise Cloud and GHES). Fix: in GitHub > Organisation Settings > Developer settings > Personal access tokens, regenerate with `admin:org` and `audit_log` scopes. For GitHub Enterprise Cloud, verify the organisation has enabled audit log streaming. For GitHub Team/Free plans: the audit log API is not available — remove Branch 3 from the SPL.

• **SonarQube webhook returns HEC 400 error.** The SonarQube webhook payload is not in HEC-compatible format. SonarQube webhooks send the analysis result as a JSON body, not wrapped in `{"event": ...}`. Fix: configure HEC to accept raw JSON on the `/services/collector/raw` endpoint, or add a middleware (Lambda, Cloud Function) that wraps the payload. Check `$SPLUNK_HOME/var/log/splunk/splunkd.log` for `HttpInputHandler` errors.

• **`disclosure_status` values don't match the SPL filter.** The SPL checks `disclosure_status != "closed"`. If the CSV uses different status values (e.g., "Closed", "CLOSED", "resolved"), the filter will include them as "not closed." Fix: normalise the CSV to lowercase, or make the SPL case-insensitive: `where lower(disclosure_status)!="closed"`.

• **SonarQube `project_key` contains colons (Maven convention).** SonarQube projects for Java/Maven typically use `com.example:service-a` as the `project_key`. This format doesn't match GitHub repository names. Fix: create a mapping lookup `sonar_to_repo.csv` with `project_key, repository`. Or configure SonarQube to use simple project keys matching the repository name.

• **GitHub audit event `repos_touched` threshold triggers for DevOps engineer.** A DevOps engineer updating branch protection rules across all repositories (legitimate infrastructure task) triggers `repos_touched > 15`. Fix: (a) add the engineer's username to `nis2_secdv_kfp_exceptions.csv` with `exception_until` set to the end of the change window. (b) For recurring scheduled changes, create a lookup `scheduled_admin_windows.csv` with `actor, start_date, end_date` and filter: `| lookup scheduled_admin_windows.csv actor OUTPUT end_date AS admin_window | where isnull(admin_window) OR now() > strptime(admin_window, "%Y-%m-%d")`.

• **Append result explosion — too many SonarQube findings.** If your SonarQube instance has thousands of open BLOCKER/CRITICAL findings across hundreds of projects, the append may produce a very large result set. Fix: add a threshold to the SonarQube branch: `| where open_findings > 5` to focus on projects with significant backlogs. Or limit to NIS2-scoped projects: `| lookup nis2_scoped_repositories.csv project_key OUTPUT in_scope | where in_scope="yes"`.

• **Duplicate `disclosure_id` entries in the CSV.** If the VD coordinator accidentally adds the same disclosure twice, it will appear as two overdue rows. Fix: add `| dedup disclosure_id` after the `inputlookup` to keep only the first row per disclosure.
grandmaExplanation: We watch whether software checks and safety nets for new code are really happening, and whether outside researchers who report problems get timely answers. If something important slips, we point to the right engineering or legal owner.
dataModelAcceleration: Optional Alerts model tagging; otherwise keep explicit sourcetype filters for performance.
equipmentModels: Splunk Add-on for GitHub (Splunkbase 5598, organisation audit log events)
SonarQube Server (webhook integration via HEC for issue data)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, optional Alerts data model tagging)
Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud (HEC for SonarQube and VD intake data)
